title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent 403s are frustrating, especially when permissions haven't changed.

To help me understand the context: can you walk me through what a typical session looks like when this happens? For instance, are you hitting the dashboard fresh after logging in, or are you already working in TaskFlow and navigating back to it? And when you say it started a couple days ago, did anything change around that time — a deploy, a team change, a browser update, anything at all that you noticed, even if it seems unrelated?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Usually I just log in fresh in the morning and go to the dashboard. Sometimes it works, sometimes I get the 403 right away. If I refresh the page it might work on the next try. I don't think anything changed on my end — though I was recently given the 'analyst' role, not sure if that matters. I think there might have been some kind of deployment or update around the time it started, but I'm not 100% sure on that. I honestly figured it was network issues since it's so inconsistent.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: That 'analyst' role detail is really interesting — the timing feels like it could be connected. Can you tell me more about when you were given that role relative to when the 403s started? And the teammates who are also seeing this — do you know if they're also on the 'analyst' role, or is it a mix of different roles? I'm curious whether this is something specific to how that role interacts with the dashboard.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I got the analyst role maybe a day or two before the errors started, so yeah pretty close timing. As for my teammates — I think the ones who mentioned having the same issue are also analysts, now that you mention it. I haven't heard complaints from the folks who are admins or editors. Could the analyst role be broken or something? It's weird though because it works sometimes, so it can't be completely wrong... right?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
This is really helpful — I think a picture is forming. Let me share what I'm seeing and you can tell me if it sounds right:

It looks like the 403s are tied to the **analyst role**, not to your account or network. The timing lines up (role assigned → errors start), and it's only analysts who are affected. The intermittent part is the interesting wrinkle — it suggests the permissions aren't *completely* wrong, but something is inconsistently evaluating them (maybe a cache, maybe different servers handling different requests).

Does that match your experience? And one more thing that would really help the team investigate: do you know if the 'analyst' role is brand new (as in, recently created for your team), or is it a role that's existed for a while and you were just newly assigned to it? That distinction would tell us whether to look at the role's definition or at how it's being evaluated.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah that sounds about right to me. The analyst role is brand new — they just created it recently for our team. Before that we didn't have it at all. So it's definitely a new role, not one I was just added to. And yeah, your theory about different servers or something makes sense I guess — it really does feel like roughly every third request fails, now that I think about it. Like if I refresh a couple times it usually works on the second or third try.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 Forbidden on dashboard for users with the new 'analyst' role","problem":"Users assigned the newly created 'analyst' role receive intermittent 403 Forbidden errors when accessing the TaskFlow dashboard. The errors began shortly after the role was created and assigned. Non-analyst roles (admin, editor) are unaffected. Refreshing typically succeeds within 2-3 attempts.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The new 'analyst' role is not consistently recognized across all application instances or authorization evaluation paths. Most likely causes: (1) a permissions cache that some instances have refreshed and others haven't, (2) a multi-server deployment where the role definition or dashboard access grant wasn't propagated to all nodes, or (3) an eventually-consistent authorization store where the new role's permission entries are intermittently unavailable.","reproduction_steps":["Create or use an account assigned only the 'analyst' role","Log in and navigate to the dashboard","Observe that some requests return 403 while others succeed","Repeat 5-10 times to confirm the ~1-in-3 failure pattern","Compare with an admin or editor account on the same dashboard — no 403s expected"],"environment":"Affects all users with the 'analyst' role. Role was recently created. Multiple team members affected. No client-side or network cause identified. Production environment, likely multi-instance deployment.","severity":"high","impact":"All users with the new 'analyst' role are intermittently locked out of the dashboard. This blocks the analyst team from reliably using TaskFlow and erodes trust in the permissions system.","recommended_fix":"1. Check the role/permission definition for 'analyst' — verify it includes dashboard access grants. 2. Inspect whether the authorization system uses caching and whether new roles require cache invalidation across all instances. 3. Check server logs correlating 403 responses with the requesting instance/node to confirm inconsistency across instances. 4. If using a distributed permission store, verify replication status for the analyst role entries. 5. As an immediate mitigation, restart or force-refresh permission caches across all application instances.","proposed_test_case":"Create an integration test that: (1) creates a new role with dashboard access, (2) assigns it to a test user, (3) makes N sequential dashboard requests across all application instances (or via round-robin), and (4) asserts that 100% of requests succeed with no 403s. This validates that new role propagation is consistent.","information_gaps":["Exact authorization architecture (caching layer, number of instances, permission store type)","Whether a deployment occurred around the same time the role was created","Server-side logs showing which instances return 403 vs 200 for analyst users"]}
